text: The surgeon performs adhesiolysis to identify the herniated root limb, carefully separating adhesions to expose the anatomical structures involved.
The surgeon frees the gastrojejunal anastomosis from its mediastinal attachments, completing the dissection and separation of these structures.
The surgeon performs a posterior cruroplasty, reinforcing the posterior aspect of the diaphragmatic crura.
The surgeon focuses on the left diaphragm, identifying the buccal lac hernia defect.
The surgeon reduces the omentum and colon back into the abdominal cavity, reversing their herniated position.
The surgeon performs sharp and blunt lysis of adhesions, exposing the diaphragm musculature, revealing its structure and surrounding tissue.
The surgeon performs a primary repair of the buccal lac hernia defect using non-absorbable sutures, securing the tissue in place.
The surgeon places a composite mesh in an onlay fashion over the repaired area for reinforcement.
The surgeon places a bioabsorbable mesh over the hiatal hernia repair site, reinforcing the area with visible precision.
